title: Complex education graph
summary: A nuanced argument about hybrid lecture attendance with multiple supports, counterarguments, and rebuttals.

nodes.content: Universities should adopt hybrid attendance policies for large lecture courses.
Hybrid attendance improves access for commuting, working, and immunocompromised students.
A campus survey found that 61 percent of students missed at least one lecture because of work or transportation conflicts.
Recorded lectures help students review difficult material and improve retention.
A learning sciences study reported higher quiz performance when students could revisit lecture recordings before assessments.
Hybrid policy is better than either mandatory attendance or fully remote delivery because it preserves flexibility and course continuity.
If students can stay home, attendance and campus engagement may decline sharply.
Participation credit, required in-person checkpoints, and discussion sections can preserve engagement while keeping hybrid flexibility.
A neighboring university kept lecture attendance steady after tying weekly discussion participation to course credit.
Hybrid delivery can widen inequity if some students lack reliable devices or internet access.
Device-lending programs and on-campus quiet study spaces can reduce most access barriers.
The university library loaned hotspots and laptops to more than 400 students last semester, but demand still exceeded supply during finals week.
assumptions: Would expanding the lending program be financially sustainable?
